title: Cycle of the Werewolf - The Ran(King) of King - Part 7
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3iUH-OYuGIo

Daniel (@dungeondive) continues The Ran(King) of King with 1983's Cycle of the Werewolf — an illustrated novella with words by King and exquisite black-and-white art by Bernie Wrightson. Begun as a calendar project, it grew into a roughly 120-page book where a werewolf haunts the small town of Tarker's Mills, claiming a new victim under each month's full moon — one chapter per month, preserving that calendar structure.

Daniel is candid that the story is slight — pure genre, with only flickers of King's usual depth (mainly the July chapter, which fleshes out wheelchair-bound young hero Marty Coslaw). What he keeps returning to is Wrightson's artwork: gorgeous, gruesome line work that thrilled him as a gore-hungry kid and still wows him. He argues this is a true graphic novel in the purest sense — he wouldn't want to read it without the art — and uses it to highlight King's mastery of form and structure, contrasting its month-by-month chunking with the unbroken, chapterless tension of Cujo. Immensely readable and one he rereads every couple of years for its brevity.

On the board it lands mid-pack — above The Tommyknockers, below Skeleton Crew.

----

Does Bernie Wrightson's art carry Cycle of the Werewolf for you, or does the slight story leave it as minor King?
